past_medical_history: Migraine without aura diagnosed at age 17, episodic, approximately 2-3 per month
Generalized anxiety disorder diagnosed at age 20
Iron deficiency anemia treated and resolved at age 22
Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo episode at age 23, resolved with Epley maneuver

medications.frequency: As needed for migraine, approximately 2-3 times per month

family_history: Mother age 52 with migraine headaches and hypothyroidism
Father age 54 with type 2 diabetes and hypertension
Maternal aunt with systemic lupus erythematosus
Paternal grandfather with Parkinson disease, onset at age 72
No known family history of multiple sclerosis or other demyelinating disease

social_history.occupation: Graduate student in environmental science, currently struggling with academic workload due to fatigue
social_history.alcohol: Social drinker, 2-3 drinks per week, mostly on weekends
social_history.recreational_drugs: Occasional marijuana use in the past, none in the last year
social_history.living_situation: Lives with a roommate in an apartment near university campus
social_history.exercise: Previously very active with running and swimming; has cut back significantly over the past 6 months due to fatigue and occasional unsteadiness
social_history.driving: Drives daily but has had two near-miss incidents she attributes to momentary dizziness

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Appears fatigued but cooperative and provides a reliable history. Speech is fluent without paraphasic errors. Language comprehension and repetition intact. Attention intact with digit span 7 forward, 5 backward. Memory 3/3 registration, 3/3 recall at 5 minutes. Clock drawing normal. Mini-Mental State Examination score 29/30 (lost one point on serial sevens, attributed to anxiety). No evidence of cognitive impairment beyond what might be expected from fatigue and anxiety.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light, 3 mm to 2 mm bilaterally. Visual acuity 20/20 bilaterally with correction. Visual fields full to confrontation. Fundoscopic exam unremarkable bilaterally, no disc pallor or papilledema. Extraocular movements full. Subtle end-gaze horizontal nystagmus on extreme leftward gaze that is not sustained and is difficult to reproduce consistently. Facial sensation intact. Face symmetric with normal strength. Hearing intact bilaterally to finger rub. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius 5/5. Tongue midline.
neurological_exam.motor: Normal bulk throughout. Tone normal in upper and lower extremities bilaterally. Strength 5/5 in all major muscle groups of the upper and lower extremities. No pronator drift. No tremor or fasciculations.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact throughout. Vibration sense intact at great toes bilaterally. Proprioception intact at great toes. No sensory level identified. Temperature sensation intact. No extinction on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ at biceps, triceps, and brachioradialis bilaterally. Patellar reflexes 2+ bilaterally. Achilles reflexes 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No Hoffman sign.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose without dysmetria bilaterally. Heel-to-shin without dysmetria. Rapid alternating movements intact bilaterally.
neurological_exam.gait: Normal stride and base width. Tandem gait intact though she notes subjective unsteadiness. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: Uhthoff phenomenon not elicited in the clinic. Patient reports that her symptoms consistently worsen after hot showers or vigorous exercise, which she has attributed to deconditioning and anxiety. Lhermitte sign not tested as there is no clinical suspicion of cervical spine pathology at presentation.

patient.chief_complaint: Recurrent episodes of vertigo, progressive fatigue, and intermittent tingling in the hands over the past 8 months, previously attributed to anxiety and migraine
patient.history_present_illness:
Ms. Kowalski is a 25-year-old right-handed graduate student who was admitted for further evaluation of an 8-month history of episodic vertigo, progressive fatigue, and intermittent paresthesias that have been attributed to anxiety and migraine by her primary care physician and a prior neurologist. The patient reports that her symptoms began approximately 8 months ago when she developed sudden-onset room-spinning vertigo that lasted about 3 days and then gradually resolved over a week. She was seen in the emergency department at that time, diagnosed with a recurrence of benign paroxysmal positional vertigo despite the Dix-Hallpike test being equivocal, and was discharged with meclizine. She saw her primary care physician in follow-up who noted she appeared anxious and attributed residual lightheadedness to her generalized anxiety disorder.

Approximately 4 months ago, she developed tingling and numbness in the fingertips of both hands that she noticed primarily in the morning and which would come and go throughout the day. She was evaluated by her primary care physician who checked basic blood work including a CBC and metabolic panel, both of which were normal, and told her the symptoms were likely related to anxiety or possibly carpal tunnel syndrome from long hours typing her thesis. She was given wrist splints which she wore intermittently without clear benefit.

Over the past 2 months, she has noticed a profound and worsening fatigue unlike anything she has experienced before. She describes it as an overwhelming sense of exhaustion that is not proportional to her activity level and is not relieved by sleep. She sleeps 9 to 10 hours per night but wakes feeling unrefreshed. Her academic performance has suffered significantly. She has also noticed that her symptoms, particularly the tingling and a vague sense of imbalance, seem to worsen noticeably after hot showers, which she has attributed to orthostatic hypotension or deconditioning.

Three weeks ago, she experienced a second distinct episode of vertigo lasting approximately 2 days, associated with nausea and mild gait unsteadiness. Her roommate noticed that she seemed to have difficulty walking in a straight line during this episode. She was seen by a neurologist who ordered a brain MRI which showed a few scattered non-specific white matter lesions. The neurologist told her these were likely related to migraine and anxiety, noting that white matter changes can be seen in young women with migraine. However, her primary care physician was not entirely reassured and referred her for inpatient evaluation to a tertiary center for a second opinion.

The patient herself is skeptical that anything serious is going on and has been told by multiple providers that her symptoms are anxiety-related. She admits that the escalating symptoms have increased her anxiety, creating what she perceives as a vicious cycle.

findings.location: Left subcortical parietal lobe, near the centrum semiovale
Right periventricular region adjacent to the body of the lateral ventricle

additional_observations: Ventricles and sulci are normal in size and configuration for age
No midline shift or mass effect
Major intracranial vessels demonstrate normal flow voids
No restricted diffusion to suggest acute ischemia
mri.impression: Few scattered non-specific supratentorial white matter T2/FLAIR hyperintensities without enhancement, restricted diffusion, or mass effect. The distribution is predominantly subcortical with two small periventricular foci. In a 25-year-old woman with history of migraine, the differential includes migraine-related white matter changes, small vessel disease (unlikely given age), and early demyelinating disease, though the latter is considered less likely given the absence of classic ovoid periventricular morphology, enhancement, or infratentorial/juxtacortical lesions meeting McDonald criteria. Clinical correlation recommended.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Non-specific white matter lesions of uncertain significance

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Less likely based on current imaging alone

recommended_actions: Consider CSF analysis if demyelinating disease remains a concern
Follow-up MRI in 3-6 months may be considered to assess for interval change

labs.interpretation: All laboratory values within normal limits. CBC, BMP, thyroid function, inflammatory markers, ANA, vitamin B12, and folate are all unremarkable. No evidence of systemic inflammatory process, thyroid dysfunction, or nutritional deficiency to explain the patient's symptoms.

special_tests.oligoclonal_bands: Not detected in CSF or serum

csf.interpretation: CSF analysis is entirely within normal limits. Notably, oligoclonal bands are not detected, and the IgG index is normal. While the absence of oligoclonal bands does not definitively exclude multiple sclerosis, it is present in approximately 90-95% of confirmed MS cases. These findings are more consistent with a non-inflammatory etiology or very early disease. Normal protein and glucose with no pleocytosis argue against active CNS inflammation, infection, or malignancy.

Visual_Evoked_Potentials.clinical_significance: Normal P100 latency, no evidence of optic nerve demyelination on the left
Mildly prolonged P100 latency suggesting subtle right optic nerve conduction delay, possibly consistent with subclinical optic nerve demyelination
Abnormal interocular latency difference suggesting asymmetric optic nerve conduction, which can be seen in subclinical demyelinating optic neuropathy

output.interpretation: Visual evoked potentials show a mildly prolonged P100 latency on the right (118 ms, upper limit of normal 115 ms) with a significant interocular latency difference of 10 ms (normal <6 ms). The left eye is normal. These findings suggest subtle right optic nerve conduction delay that could be consistent with subclinical demyelination. However, the prolongation is mild and could also be seen as a borderline finding. Clinical correlation is recommended.
abnormal_values_summary: Mildly prolonged P100 latency right eye (118 ms, normal <115 ms)
Abnormal interocular latency difference (10 ms, normal <6 ms)

findings.location: Cervical spinal cord at C3-C4 level, eccentric to the dorsolateral right cord
findings.size: 8 mm in craniocaudal extent, spanning less than one vertebral segment

additional_observations: Remainder of the cervical cord appears normal in signal and caliber
Thoracic spinal cord is unremarkable without signal abnormality
No leptomeningeal enhancement in the spine
No disc herniations or significant spondylosis
Conus medullaris terminates at normal level without signal abnormality
output.impression: Single short-segment T2 hyperintense lesion in the dorsolateral right cervical cord at C3-C4 without enhancement or cord expansion. This lesion is peripherally located and short in segment, which is more typical of a demyelinating lesion than a vascular or neoplastic etiology. In conjunction with the known supratentorial white matter lesions, this finding raises the possibility of a demyelinating process such as multiple sclerosis with dissemination in space involving both brain and spinal cord. The absence of enhancement suggests the lesion is not acutely active. Longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (spanning 3+ segments) is not seen, making NMOSD less likely.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Possible but less likely given peripheral location
Less likely without clinical history of preceding infection

recommended_actions: Correlate with brain MRI findings for assessment of dissemination in space
Consider repeat imaging in 3 months to assess for new lesions (dissemination in time)
CSF analysis for oligoclonal bands if not yet performed

OCT_RNFL.clinical_significance: Borderline thin RNFL in the right eye, at the 5th percentile for age. Could indicate early subclinical axonal loss in the right optic nerve.
Thinning in temporal quadrant of right eye, the sector most affected by optic nerve demyelination

OCT_RNFL.test: Macular ganglion cell layer - Right eye
OCT_RNFL.clinical_significance: Mild ganglion cell layer thinning in the right eye, supporting subclinical optic nerve pathology

OCT_RNFL.test: Macular ganglion cell layer - Left eye

output.interpretation: OCT demonstrates borderline thinning of the right eye RNFL, particularly in the temporal quadrant (58 um, below the normal range of 65-95 um), with associated mild macular ganglion cell layer thinning. The left eye is normal. These findings are subtle and would not typically be detected on routine ophthalmologic examination. In the context of prolonged VEP latency on the right, these findings support subclinical right optic nerve pathology, possibly from prior subclinical demyelination. However, the changes are mild and could fall within the range of normal anatomic variation in isolation.
abnormal_values_summary: Borderline thin average RNFL right eye (88 um, lower limit 90 um)
Temporal quadrant RNFL thinning right eye (58 um, normal 65-95 um)
Mild ganglion cell layer thinning right eye (76 um, normal 80-100 um)

NMO_Antibody_Panel.clinical_significance: Negative AQP4 antibody effectively excludes NMOSD in most cases (sensitivity ~75-80% with cell-based assay)
Negative MOG antibody argues against MOG antibody disease (MOGAD)

output.interpretation: Both aquaporin-4 and MOG antibodies are negative, making NMOSD and MOGAD unlikely diagnoses. These negative results, combined with the clinical and imaging picture, shift the differential further toward multiple sclerosis or non-inflammatory etiologies.

findings.location: Right periventricular white matter, oriented perpendicular to the lateral ventricle with ovoid morphology suggestive of Dawson finger
Left inferior frontal gyrus, involving the U-fibers

additional_observations: Previously seen subcortical occipital lesion is no longer clearly visualized, possibly resolved
The cervical cord lesion at C3-C4 persists, unchanged
Two new lesions demonstrate enhancement, consistent with recent inflammatory activity
output.impression: Comparison with prior MRI from 3 months ago demonstrates two new supratentorial white matter lesions with gadolinium enhancement: (1) a new 7 mm ovoid periventricular lesion with incomplete ring enhancement oriented perpendicular to the lateral ventricle (classic Dawson finger morphology), and (2) a new 4 mm juxtacortical lesion in the left inferior frontal gyrus with faint nodular enhancement. The previously seen lesions are stable. These new enhancing lesions, combined with the previously identified non-enhancing lesions and the cervical cord lesion, now satisfy the 2017 McDonald criteria for dissemination in space (periventricular, juxtacortical, and spinal cord) and dissemination in time (coexistence of enhancing and non-enhancing lesions). The findings are now highly consistent with relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Unlikely given lesion morphology and negative AQP4
Unlikely given typical MS morphology of new lesions

recommended_actions: Initiate disease-modifying therapy for relapsing-remitting MS
Refer to MS specialist for long-term management
Repeat MRI in 6-12 months to assess treatment response

SSEP.test: Median nerve SSEP - Right N20 latency
Median nerve SSEP - Left N20 latency
Tibial nerve SSEP - Right P37 latency
Tibial nerve SSEP - Left P37 latency
SSEP.clinical_significance: Borderline but within normal limits; may warrant follow-up if clinical suspicion remains high

output.interpretation: Somatosensory evoked potentials are within normal limits for both upper and lower extremities bilaterally. Left tibial nerve P37 latency is borderline (43.8 ms, upper limit 44 ms). These findings do not demonstrate definite central conduction delay but do not exclude subclinical demyelination given normal values can be seen early in the disease course.

output.query: multiple sclerosis diagnosis oligoclonal bands negative

results.title: Oligoclonal band negative multiple sclerosis: clinical and demographic features, disease course, and prognosis
results.summary: Approximately 5-10% of patients with clinically definite MS have persistently negative oligoclonal bands. These patients may have a milder disease course and are more likely to have primary progressive MS.

results.title: The 2017 McDonald Criteria for the Diagnosis of Multiple Sclerosis
results.summary: CSF oligoclonal bands can substitute for dissemination in time in the 2017 McDonald criteria, but their absence does not exclude the diagnosis if DIT is demonstrated by other means such as new lesions on follow-up MRI.

results.title: CSF findings in patients with MS and negative OCB
results.summary: OCB-negative MS patients tend to present with milder CSF inflammatory profiles and may require more rigorous MRI follow-up to establish dissemination in time for diagnosis.

output.summary: Oligoclonal bands are absent in approximately 5-10% of confirmed MS cases. The 2017 McDonald criteria do not require positive OCB for diagnosis if dissemination in space and time can be demonstrated by MRI criteria alone. OCB-negative MS may be associated with a milder phenotype. Diagnosis should not be excluded solely on the basis of negative OCB.

differential.key_features: History of migraine, subcortical lesion distribution, non-specific morphology on initial MRI, young female patient
Episodic neurological symptoms with CNS white matter lesions, but negative AQP4 antibodies, absence of longitudinally extensive myelitis, and short-segment cord lesion argue strongly against
Young female with CNS demyelination, but negative MOG antibodies and atypical presentation for MOGAD
White matter lesions could theoretically represent vasculitic changes, but normal ESR/CRP, normal CSF, and absence of systemic symptoms argue against

differential.likelihood: Initially considered primary diagnosis by referring providers
differential.key_features: Known anxiety disorder, symptoms attributed to psychosomatic cause, but does not explain white matter lesions, VEP abnormalities, or spinal cord lesion

optimal_actions.action: Perform detailed neurological history focusing on prior episodes that could represent relapses, including temporal pattern and heat sensitivity (Uhthoff phenomenon)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: History reveals episodic vertigo, paresthesias at different times, and worsening with heat — suggesting relapsing course with Uhthoff phenomenon

optimal_actions.action: Perform complete neurological examination including assessment for subtle findings such as internuclear ophthalmoplegia, nystagmus, and Lhermitte sign
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Subtle end-gaze nystagmus noted; other findings may be normal or equivocal in this diagnostic puzzle

optimal_actions.action: Review the brain MRI with attention to lesion morphology and distribution relative to McDonald criteria for dissemination in space
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Non-specific white matter lesions, predominantly subcortical, with some periventricular foci; does not clearly meet McDonald DIS criteria on initial imaging

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI of cervical and thoracic spine with contrast to evaluate for spinal cord lesions
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Short-segment dorsolateral cervical cord lesion at C3-C4, typical of demyelination, adding spinal cord as a DIS location

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture with CSF analysis including oligoclonal bands and IgG index
optimal_actions.expected_finding: CSF entirely normal with negative oligoclonal bands — a diagnostically challenging finding present in 5-10% of MS cases

optimal_actions.action: Order visual evoked potentials to assess for subclinical optic nerve involvement
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mildly prolonged P100 latency on right with abnormal interocular latency difference suggesting subclinical optic nerve demyelination

optimal_actions.action: Order OCT to evaluate retinal nerve fiber layer for evidence of prior subclinical optic neuritis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Borderline RNFL thinning in right eye temporal quadrant, supporting subclinical optic nerve pathology

optimal_actions.action: Order aquaporin-4 and MOG antibody testing to exclude NMOSD and MOGAD
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Both antibodies negative, excluding NMOSD and MOGAD

optimal_actions.action: Plan follow-up MRI in 3 months to assess for dissemination in time with new or enhancing lesions
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Two new enhancing lesions including a classic Dawson finger, now meeting full McDonald 2017 criteria for RRMS

optimal_actions.action: Initiate disease-modifying therapy and refer to MS specialist for long-term management
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Treatment initiation with appropriate DMT after confirmed diagnosis

critical_actions: Do not dismiss white matter lesions as migraine-related without adequate workup for demyelinating disease
Order spine MRI to assess for spinal cord lesions contributing to dissemination in space
Order VEP to evaluate for subclinical optic nerve involvement given the otherwise non-specific initial presentation
Plan follow-up MRI to demonstrate dissemination in time given the negative OCB and equivocal initial imaging
Test for AQP4 and MOG antibodies to exclude important mimics before diagnosing MS
Recognize that negative oligoclonal bands do not exclude MS (5-10% of cases are OCB-negative)
contraindicated_actions: Diagnosing migraine-related white matter changes without adequate exclusion of demyelinating disease
Starting disease-modifying therapy before the diagnosis is confirmed with adequate evidence
Performing brain biopsy when the clinical and imaging picture can be resolved with follow-up imaging
Attributing all symptoms to anxiety without investigating the objective MRI findings
Starting corticosteroids empirically without establishing a diagnosis first (unless acute relapse warrants it)
key_reasoning_points: Episodic vertigo and paresthesias occurring at different times represent potential MS relapses disseminated in time, even though they were attributed to BPPV and anxiety
Worsening of symptoms after hot showers (Uhthoff phenomenon) is a classic MS feature that was misattributed to deconditioning
Initial MRI shows non-specific white matter lesions that overlap with migraine-related changes, but the presence of periventricular lesions in a young woman warrants further investigation for MS
Negative oligoclonal bands occur in 5-10% of confirmed MS cases and should not be used to exclude the diagnosis
The cervical cord lesion on spine MRI adds a critical dissemination-in-space location and has a morphology typical of demyelination (short segment, dorsolateral, peripheral)
Subtle VEP prolongation and OCT RNFL thinning in the right eye provide paraclinical evidence of subclinical optic nerve involvement, a third site supporting dissemination in space
The follow-up MRI at 3 months demonstrates new enhancing lesions with classic MS morphology, providing definitive evidence of dissemination in time and confirming the diagnosis
The diagnostic challenge lies in recognizing that a seemingly reassuring initial workup (normal exam, non-specific MRI, normal CSF) does not exclude MS when the clinical history is suggestive

metadata.key_diagnostic_challenge: OCB-negative MS with non-specific initial MRI resembling migraine-related white matter changes; diagnosis requires persistent clinical suspicion, multimodal paraclinical testing, and follow-up imaging to demonstrate dissemination in time
metadata.teaching_points: Not all MS presents with classic findings. Negative OCB, atypical MRI, and normal neurological exam can occur. Uhthoff phenomenon and episodic neurological symptoms disseminated in time and space should raise suspicion even when initial workup is equivocal.
